text: The surgeon demonstrates a single port superpubic total abdominal colectomy with ileorectal anastomosis. A three-centimeter fan and steel incision is made for the single port access system, and a wound protector is inserted. Using a 10mm, 30-degree bariatric scope, the surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle and begins a medial to lateral dissection into the retroperitoneal plane with a Park's grasper in the left hand and a ligature in the right hand.

The surgeon demonstrates the setup for a single port superpubic total abdominal colectomy with iliorectal anastomosis. A three-centimeter fan and steel incision is made, and a wound protector is inserted. The surgeon uses a ten millimeter, thirty degree bariatric scope and employs a Park's grasper in the left hand and a ligature in the right hand for the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle and performs a medial to lateral dissection, entering the retroperitoneal plane. A window is created around the ileocolic pedicle, which is then divided using a ligature. The ureter is visible during the procedure. The surgeon lifts the right colon mesentery off the retroperitoneum, avoiding high ligation due to the benign nature of the disease.

The surgeon creates a window around the iliocolic pedicle and divides it using a ligature device. The ureter is visible nearby. The right colon mesentery is lifted off the retroperitoneum, with the dissection proceeding closer to the colon without high ligation due to the benign nature of the disease.

The surgeon adjusts the port to change instrument angles, facilitating dissection. Using a 30-degree camera, the duodenum is swept down off the transverse colon mesentery. The mesentery is taken without high ligations, revealing the inferior vena cava and kidney, with minimal retroperitoneal fat visible. The right colic artery is identified and triply cauterized for secure control.

The surgeon adjusts the single port to change the instrument angles, facilitating the dissection. The duodenum is swept down off the transverse colon mesentery using a 30-degree camera angle. The mesentery is taken without high ligations, revealing the inferior vena cava and kidney with minimal retroperitoneal fat. The right colic artery is identified and triply cauterized for secure hemostasis.

The surgeon gently teases the duodenum down, working with straight instruments through a single port located one centimeter above the pubic bone. The right branch of the middle colic artery is identified and dissected up to the colon wall. The surgeon then changes trajectory to focus on the hepatic colic ligament, preparing to enter the dissection plane from below.

The surgeon is positioned one centimeter above the pubic bone, using straight instruments to dissect the right branch of the middle colic artery up to the colon wall. The medial dissection of the right colon and hepatic flexure is complete, and the surgeon transitions to dissecting the hepatic colic ligament from below, ensuring the duodenum is safely swept down to avoid accidental cautery.

The surgeon stays close to the colon, having swept the duodenum down, and enters the previously dissected medial plane. Bruising is visible as the plane comes into view. The surgeon navigates around the hepatic flexure, using traction with the left hand and hugging the bowel wall with the ligature. Lateral attachments to the hepatic flexure are taken down, and dissection continues along the white line down to the cecum. The ureter is shown, and the surgeon leaves the cecal and terminal ileum attachments intact to prevent the colon from twisting during patient position changes.

The surgeon navigates the previously dissected medial plane, maneuvering around the hepatic flexure with traction applied using the left hand and the ligature hugging the bowel wall. The lateral attachments to the hepatic flexure are taken down, and the dissection continues along the white line down to the cecum. The ureter is visible, and the surgeon leaves the cecal and terminal ileum attachments intact to prevent the colon from twisting during patient position changes.

The surgeon proceeds to the next phase of the operation, taking the transverse colon mesentery along with the omentum. The thin patient anatomy facilitates this process. The surgeon stays close to the bowel wall, aided by the superpubic camera view and patient positioning in reverse Trendelenburg with the left shoulder elevated. As the surgeon approaches the flexure, the omental attachments are taken separately from the mesentery, allowing sequential pulling down of the tissues. The ligature is used to ensure thorough burning and prevent bleeding at the operation's farthest extent.

The surgeon uses a ligature to take the omental attachments to the transverse colon separately from the mesentery, ensuring thorough cauterization to prevent bleeding. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with the left shoulder elevated to enhance exposure. The camera is positioned superpubically, providing clear visualization as the surgeon stays close to the bowel wall while dissecting towards the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon uses single port surgery to dissect the splenic flexure, removing attachments from the inferior border of the pancreas. The splenic flexure is nearly completely mobilized, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect the descending colon mesentery, hugging the bowel wall. As the dissection progresses towards the sigmoid colon, the surgeon switches to a Trendelenburg position to improve visualization and angles, releasing lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon and continuing to mobilize the mesentery.

The surgeon uses single port surgery to carefully dissect the splenic flexure, removing attachments from the inferior border of the pancreas. The flexure is fully mobilized, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect the descending colon mesentery, maintaining close proximity to the bowel wall. As the dissection progresses from superior to inferior, visibility and instrument angles become challenging, prompting a switch in approach.

The surgeon places the patient back in Trendelenburg position and begins releasing the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon, moving from the rectosigmoid area upwards to join the previous dissection from the superior side. The challenging angle from the superior side is bypassed by approaching from the inferior side. The surgeon mobilizes the mesentery, carefully creating a window in the distal sigmoid close to the bowel wall to access and dissect through the mesentery. The lateral mobilization facilitates the dissection, allowing the surgeon to complete the sigmoid mesentery dissection, preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery.

The surgeon creates a window in the distal sigmoid mesentery close to the bowel wall, facilitating the dissection of the sigmoid mesentery. The lateral mobilization aids in completing the colon mesentery dissection down to the rectosigmoid. The superior hemorrhoidal artery is preserved. The surgeon plans to address the lateral attachments of the cecum, terminal ileum, and appendix last to prevent twisting of the colon or small bowel, ensuring the iliocolic junction can be divided without complications. The medial dissection is joined, and the colon is fully mobilized.

The surgeon completes the lateral attachments to the cecum, terminal ileum, and appendix, ensuring the colon and small bowel do not twist. The cecum is exteriorized using a Babcock clamp, and the ileocolic junction is exposed. The surgeon clears additional mesentery and divides the ileocolic junction with a stapler, placing a stitch on the ileum.

The surgeon removes the cap from the port and exteriorizes the cecum using a Babcock clamp, then exteriorizes the ileocolic junction. After clearing additional mesentery, the ileocolic junction is divided with a stapler. The divided colon is exteriorized back to the rectus sigmoid junction, which has been cleaned of its mesentery. The surgeon divides the upper rectum through a low fan and steel incision. Before making the anastomosis, the surgeon ensures there is no twist in the mesentery by positioning the small bowel to the patient's left and verifying the mesentery alignment from the duodenum to the cut edge of the ileum.

The surgeon has exteriorized the divided colon back to the rectosigmoid junction, which is completely cleared of its mesentery. The division occurs in the upper rectum, facilitated by a low Pfannenstiel incision. The surgeon ensures the small bowel is positioned to the patient's left, checking for any twists in the mesentery before proceeding with the anastomosis. The ileum is adjusted to eliminate a twist, revealing the cut edge of the small bowel mesentery extending from the duodenum straight down to the ileocolic pedicle.

The surgeon completes a hand-sewn side-to-end anastomosis through the fan and steel incision, ensuring the mesentery is straight. The ileum is clamped, and the pelvis is flooded for a leak test, which is negative.

The surgeon completes a hand-sewn side-to-end anastomosis through the fan and steel incision, ensuring the mesentery is straight. The ileum is clamped, and the pelvis is flooded for a leak test, which is negative, confirming the integrity of the anastomosis.
